facts.size: About 38–47 cm — the largest member of the kingfisher family
facts.habitat: Open woodland, forest edges, and suburban gardens
facts.diet: Snakes, lizards, insects, and small rodents

hints.region: Native to eastern Australia and introduced to Western Australia and New Zealand — one of Australia's most iconic bird calls
hints.size: About 42 cm — the world's largest kingfisher, stocky and broad-headed
hints.behavior: Starts each day with a loud, cackling call that sounds like hysterical laughter — family groups call together in a 'chorus' to declare their territory at dawn and dusk

funFact: Kookaburras kill snakes by snatching them behind the head and slamming them against a hard surface, or dropping them from height, before swallowing them whole.

facts.habitat: Rainforest, woodland, heathland, and suburban gardens with flowering trees
facts.migration: Nomadic; follows flowering events across the landscape

hints.region: Common across coastal eastern Australia — noisy flocks are a familiar sight in Australian cities and parks
hints.size: About 28 cm — similar in size to a pigeon but far more colourful — vivid blue, green, red, orange, and yellow
hints.behavior: Has a specialised brush-tipped tongue for lapping up nectar from flowers; travels in noisy, fast-moving flocks and roosts communally in thousands

funFact: Rainbow Lorikeets have been observed to play — rolling around on branches, hanging upside down, and wrestling with other birds, apparently for fun.

facts.size: 150–190 cm tall; the world's second-largest living bird
facts.habitat: Scrubland, grassland, and open woodland across mainland Australia
facts.diet: Plants, seeds, fruits, insects, and grubs
facts.migration: Nomadic; travels long distances in response to rainfall and food availability

hints.region: Found across mainland Australia — the national bird emblem on the Australian coat of arms, and a familiar sight in outback Australia
hints.size: Up to 190 cm tall — the second-tallest bird in the world and a formidable runner reaching speeds of 50 km/h
hints.behavior: Completely flightless — its wings are tiny vestigial structures; the male alone incubates the eggs and raises the chicks, fasting for eight weeks without food or water

funFact: Australia fought an infamous 'Emu War' in 1932 — soldiers armed with machine guns failed to significantly reduce Emu numbers threatening Western Australian wheat farms.

facts.habitat: Open woodland, parks, farmland, and suburban areas
facts.diet: Invertebrates, insects, small lizards, and seeds

hints.region: Found across Australia and southern New Guinea — one of Australia's most common and familiar birds, particularly in cities
hints.size: About 40 cm — slightly larger than a European Magpie, with bold black-and-white plumage and a distinctive pale blue-grey bill
hints.behavior: Has one of the most complex and musical calls of any bird — groups perform loud carolling choruses, especially at dawn; notorious for swooping cyclists and pedestrians during nesting season

funFact: Australian Magpies can recognise individual human faces and have been documented holding 'grudges' against specific people who have disturbed their nests in previous years.

facts.size: About 14 cm including the long cocked tail
facts.habitat: Coastal scrub, heathland, and suburban gardens with dense low shrubs

hints.region: Common across southeastern Australia from Queensland to South Australia — a beloved backyard bird
hints.size: Tiny — about 14 cm including its long, cocked tail — with the breeding male's brilliant blue head being one of the most striking colours in Australian bird life
hints.behavior: Lives in cooperative family groups where younger males help raise the dominant pair's chicks; females still mate with males from other groups — one of the most studied examples of 'extra-pair' mating in birds

funFact: Female Superb Fairywrens teach their eggs a 'password' — a specific call note — before they hatch; chicks that include this call in their begging calls get more food, which also helps parents identify cuckoo chicks.
